speaker: interviewer
text: May 23rd, 1995. Tape 2. All right. This is now March, 1945. You are headed towards the south of France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still know nothing about what has been going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were traveling, could you see areas that had been bombed out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When-- toward the end of March, we hit Southern France. And it was going into the first or second week of April, if I can remember. It was Passover. And I heard of a small town that had just been liberated, that had been underground for almost six years. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name of the town. I really should have looked it up. I probably have it in one of my letters. And I took one of the GIs with a jeep. And we went there, because I found out that I could get some Passover supplies for our GIs, the Jewish boys, for Passover. And when I did get there, it was really-- I just couldn't believe the rejoicing that went on, that they were finally liberated. The army did bring in-- they had wine and they had matzahs. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when they talked about the joy of finally being liberated, was your reaction, liberated from what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then we-- I started to-- no. As we were going south, we started to hear more and more of what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were writing letters home all this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you also receiving letters from America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every once in a while, we would-- before I left Reims, France, we did get mail. And then traveling, we did not-- we were not able to get any until we--

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the letters you received, was there yet any evidence that anyone in the United States really understood what was going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There was not. My husband, all he knew was that he was sending me packages, which I did not receive quite a bit of them. Some I did receive. But I was writing a-- like a diary. I wrote a letter every day from the day that I left the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've saved all those, or the people to whom you  wrote them saved them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I wrote them mostly-- my mother had saved them, and I wrote to my husband. And I have them. I have the whole year of letters. And that's where most of my facts that I could look back and remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: In retrospect, in discussions with either your mother or your husband, have they ever discussed the fact that it was incredible to them or to you that no one knew what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. No, they didn't. They all-- the only news they got was the fighting that was going on. And they did not know anything about a concentration camps as yet. They just knew the-- that Hitler was fighting. There was running over so many of the countries. But nothing about concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first learn about the concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Til I walked into Dachau--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and we saw the gates of Dachau. This was May 3rd, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had not heard anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no anticipation as to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you told about what your mission was going to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told that there was a camp or something outside of Munich, and when we got there, that the people-- we were supposed to take care of the people or-- but nobody knew. They said, when we went-- when our trucks, when we went through and into the courtyard, I always say like the gates of hell opened up. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right Before we get to Dachau--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you arrive in Dachau the very first day you arrived in Germany? Did you go directly to Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- yeah. We were given-- we were told that from the Southern France, we crossed over into Germany. And we were headed for-- they said about 10 miles outside of Munich, there was a camp or people there or something that we had given.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there evidence of devastation in Germany as you crossed that border?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not-- we did not see. We got-- we got onto the Autobahn. And this is where the planes would even land and take off. And this runs practically the whole length of Germany. And riding on the Autobahn, looking at all the comments, even in our-- our unit, we kept saying, what a beautiful country. Compared to France, which was at least clean, cleanliness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany was clean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. Germany. We said-- and we kept saying, what are they fighting for? What do they want? Look at this beautiful country. Why are these people fighting?

speaker: interviewer
text: See, we did not know just what was going on. All we knew that they were fighting. For what? They wanted to rule the world, but Germany was so beautiful, you know. What did they want? All right. So you arrived--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was our reaction. And we-- and when we drove up to Dachau, as I said, when we went into Dachau, the gates there, and we came into this very, very large compound, court. One side was a-- it looked like a hotel. And we found out that the German officers stayed there. On another side, there were barracks where the German-- I guess the GIs there stayed. And then they had warehouses, which later-- a couple days later when we walked over to them and walked in, looked like they plundered Europe. You-- we found everything and anything, that-- where they accumulated all these things. There were paintings. There was one warehouse just filled with material and with cottons, spools of cotton, every conceivable type of a material that you would want. They had-- well, they had some of the-- some of the survivors there work as-- in leather goods, where they had them make shoes and had them-- of course, there were machines there with leather goods. They had-- well, there we found also some of the lampshades that Ilse Koch had taken from the skins of the people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had any other Allies arrived in Dachau before you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the soldiers that went in two days before to liberate the camp. But they just went in and, you know, they didn't stay, because they-- and we went in right after them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived, your first site was not, or was, shocking?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, we saw this compound and we saw the-- this hotel and we saw the hotel-like building. And to the right, when we went in, was this compound that was wired off with people clinging to it, yelling, carrying on, calling in all different languages. When we walked-- when we went into it, we went in with some of the GIs and the doctors, we were even told not to look too much, because they have found some of the Germans had mixed in with the people. And they were-- they knew who they were, and all of a sudden, we saw somebody hanging, you know, from a hook or whatever. We walked in some-- there were different buildings there that we walked into. And the stench was-- I'll never forget the smell of these buildings. And there were planks, three stories, if you want to-- three levels, which were like skeletons. You found the living, the half-living, and the dead among them. We walked-- we saw a building. When we went in, found it was a crematorium. And that was still filled with bodies that they had burned up. And on the outside were piles and piles of bones. We saw a building there where they gave them a cake of soap, stripped them, told them to take a shower, and they turned on the gas, the gas chambers. There was another building where there were women and children, women lying on the floor close together, babies, children that looked like they were 50 years old, with the bloated tummies from starvation. It was-- it was a horrible, horrible sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all together seeing this--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-

speaker: interviewer
text: --as a group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were a few of us that we went, started to walk through. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was-- at the time, when we came out of there and we went into this hotel-like building that was going to be our headquarters, because the army had just been through, and there were still some German officers there that were dead on the floor the GIs took out. We had to be careful of booby traps. In fact, one of the rooms, they found a box-- and I'll never forget this-- with eggs. And there were empty egg shells. And attached to the top was like a little electrical device. Now, how they got the yolk out and the tiny little hole, there were no-- and so one of the soldiers, one of the GIs, just picked it up and threw it out the window. It was a-- one of the booby traps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- they scanned the rooms pretty well. And we brought in our own cots and-- and-- and our own equipment that we stayed there. And then we had a meeting with our colonel and with everybody and the doctors. And of course, we had to start somewhere, and decide, well, the best thing to do, we had to clean out the barracks, the German barracks, because then we thought that the-- then we knew that the-- that the army would send in like double-decker beds for us. Were there any Germans alive when you arrived in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- as I said, they were the ones that were being-- not that we-- that we spoke to personally, no. It-- these would-- and they-- these were people, you have no idea the skeletons, actually skeletons, how they could walk around. There were people from all over Europe. I remember taking a couple people out of the compound. We would put them on cots to take them into the barracks there. I remember so vividly a famous-- he was a famous musician-- I don't remember his name-- at the time. And-- that he was there. And what we tried to do is to give plasma to these people. Now, if you ever saw an arm as big as the circle of your hand get into a vein-- food, we had to be careful. They could not digest meals. We would start off with a gruel, try and start with that. But before we even did that, we had to-- we had to de-louse them. There was tuberculosis. There was typhoid. There was dysentery rampant. We wore gowns. We wore masks. We had our heads covered. We wore rubber gloves. And at that time, the only thing that we knew of in 1945 was DDT powder, DDT. And we tried to take them out of the compound. And we would shave their heads and wash them down, give them a clean gown and put them into a bed and try to explain to them that they will get food. We will feed them. We gave them even bread. They would hide it under the pillow. And we told them that they didn't have to hide anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people there who could speak the language of the people in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were fortunate there. There were about 50 or 100 Russian people that had come into the camp a couple weeks before that were in very good condition. And some of them could speak English. Some of them could speak the language. They acted as our ward boys and helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were most of the inmates German?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had Polish came. We had some-- we had Hungarians. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Germans, most of them were-- I would say about 90% were the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to the Germans who were still alive when you entered who were subsequently taken out, as you said, by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear anything from the American soldiers as to how these Germans behaved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The men and children in the camp were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were also, you said, a fair number of children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the conditions vary within the camp? Did you have the feeling that any group was treated any better than any other?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: All equally terribly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the temperature at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the outside temperature? Well, it was May. It was in the 70s, 60s and 70s at that time, 70 degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had there been any medical facilities in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: None that we saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you set up everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about how you went about setting those up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, we started to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take them out of the compound. We had-- we would have-- well, our death rate was tremendous, you can imagine, maybe 100 to 150 a day, because they-- on these planks with the dead, they couldn't turn. They couldn't move. They were just living corpse. And so those that we could save, as I said, we would take them out on a stretcher and try to clean them up or see what was wrong with them as much as possible and then bring them over to the barracks that we had set up--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you decide whom to treat first?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no-- no decision. We just took as-- you know, going into the buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't a question of trying to find those who seemed to have the greatest chance of survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- no, because we didn't know. You know, you just didn't know. There were so many, so many people. We just did not know. We ourselves, when we would come off duty, or even before in the morning, we were sprayed three times a day with DDT-- we went into a dining room, there was somebody there where you go like this and get sprayed-- feeling that-- at that time, that's all we knew that would prevent us from getting any of the diseases, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were eating and sleeping where the German officers had been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How had those buildings been kept? Were they pretty neat and clean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, they were. As I said, we took everything out. We put in our own cots. The beds and everything else we did not use. But we had running water, which was unreal for us and we enjoyed that, and bathrooms. They lived very well, the German officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you estimate how many Germans were running that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really-- it was a very large camp, so there had to be a large amount. In fact, they used Dachau, where they did find some SS-- the SS people there. And you could tell them immediately. They had the SS signature under their arm. And these were the-- the Aryans. These were the master race, the blonde, blue-eyed, six foot people. And they used one building to-- as a jail, say, for them. And they were arrogant. In plain English, they were arrogant bastards. You would look at them. They'd sooner spit in your eye as look at them. And they could do no harm, and everybody was beneath them. So that was the typical SS troopers that you had to deal with.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our complement was small. We had 40 nurses. And with the GIs and the doctors, we ran maybe about 120, 130 people was an evacuation hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there differences in the reactions among the people in your company?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was just like myself, just the horror of it all. In a-- in a couple weeks, I would say, maybe in about three or four weeks, other hospitals came in to help us. It was just a humongous operation. And we worked day and night at the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the doctors at that point with you Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite a few doctors in my unit were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any difference in reaction from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, you know, like myself. You treat an animal better than what these people did.

speaker: interviewer
text: The others who subsequently came to help you, also Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were American hospital units.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And what they did, the army went into Munich. And naturally, the people all denied knowing anything about this camp 10 miles on the outskirts. And they said, you didn't see the smoke? You didn't see anything? And naturally, nobody knew anything. Well, they made them come in with wagons and take-- dig ditches, dig graves, and take these bones, take these people, and bury them, and let them see what was 10 miles from their city, what had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: The stench did not reach outside the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew-- they denied, which was just impossible, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep your composure in all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were too busy, I think. When I look back, I-- I always said it was we had too much to do. It was-- we had to help these people. They needed us so badly, we had to do what we could, just to think of ourselves or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any people who couldn't handle it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. No. We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we would talk about it and help each other and just say, we just have to take care of them, do what we could. They've been through so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how long the people had been there who were still alive when you entered the camp? Were they alive just because it had been too short a time for them to have died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because about a week before or a couple-- I think even a couple of days before, a train load had come on the rail. We had a railing in back-- in the back of the camp. And I found out that about 1,000 people were jammed into these boxcars. And when it reached Dachau, about maybe 500 people were left. The rest were dead. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when they got into Dachau, the soldiers that had gone in had opened up these boxcars and took the people that were there out. So that was a couple of days before we came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the people who had been in the camp, you said some of them were making shoes. Was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had been there maybe three, three, four years, some of them. I think it must've been about three- three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at least some of them survived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because they had a skill--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were working. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and a job to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that they-- were working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to have conversations with any of them at the time to understand what it was they had gone through or what their life had the been like or any of their experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: We really-- we really couldn't, because, as I said, there was so much to do, day and night, just trying to save them or to take care of them, and then the language difficulty and trying to make them understand what we were doing, that it was really difficult to have really stories or anything else. We just wanted to get them out of there and into the clean barracks, into a clean bed, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them show any fear in terms of thinking maybe you were tricking them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because when we went in and we started to-- well, word spread so fast, the Americanas have come, because they had seen the soldiers before. And then when we went in, you know, that they knew that it was the Americans coming to save them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you suffer any nightmares during the time you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't have time. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Since the war, have you dreamt about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's always been in back of my mind. There are certain movies that I will not see. And I did go-- in 1969, I took a trip to Israel for two weeks. And like Yad Vashem, I did not go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Holocaust Memorial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were certain places I did not go into. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went into-- two weeks in Israel, then we went into Italy. We went to Switzerland. And then we went up to Amsterdam and London. And in Amsterdam, they-- I went with the American Jewish Congress, which was a beautiful trip. They stopped at Anne Frank, and everybody went up. And I-- I stayed on the bus. I did not go up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there photographers who went in with you when you went into the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there no one documenting what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had taken-- I had a camera, and I had taken pictures. And I think before-- the soldiers that came in that liberated the camp to-- they-- they had film, because I have it on a film called And it shows going into-- the people first going into Dachau. And they it all film-- on film. They have pictures of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, let me ask you, were any of them aware ahead of time of what to expect?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every soldier you talked to, when they went into a camp they did not know what to expect. No way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- since you didn't know what to expect or anticipate, did you have enough supplies? Were supplies ever a problem for what you needed to treat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, because what we had, and then the army did, as I said-- they supplied us with the beds and the-- you know, when we cleaned out the barracks where the German soldiers stayed. And supplies came in to help. We had no problem with that. We could get supplies that we needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who provided the food for all the people in the camp by this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- as I said, the beginning, all we did-- well, we had cooks. And we made this gruel for them for a while, until they could digest, and then slowly started on a very limited diet. And like I said, our death rate was tremendous every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the greatest problems you encountered during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The disease that these people had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in addition to the starvation and just their-- their general poor health, it was the ongoing disease--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right

speaker: interviewer
text: --that might have attacked them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other problems among your own group with which you had to deal?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because we-- we cooperated among ourselves who would take a night shift or a day shift, or-- or we had to work 12 hours, 14 hours. I mean, there was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a great camaraderie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. What had to be done we did, no matter how many hours we worked or what had-- what had to be done to take care of these people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh.
